topic: Raymond pricing turquoise pieces for Gallup

turns.content: okay so i need some help with numbers today. Raymond has a bunch of turquoise pieces he is getting ready for Gallup and we are arguing about pricing. he always prices too low

Underpricing handmade work is really common, especially when the maker is close to the community they're selling in. What kind of pieces are we talking about, and what's he currently thinking for prices?

he has some rings, a couple of bolo ties, and a bracelet with kingman turquoise that is really beautiful. the bracelet he wants to price at $180. i told him that is too low. the stone alone is worth something and the hours he put in, you know. he says people will not pay more than that

He might be right about what some people will pay, but the question is whether he wants to sell to those people or to the ones who understand what handmade work is worth. A Kingman turquoise bracelet with real silver work — $180 is almost certainly below what it should be.

Can you walk me through the costs? What did the turquoise cost him, and roughly how many hours did the bracelet take?

the kingman stone he got for about $45 from a dealer he trusts. the silver sheet and wire maybe another $30 in materials. and he worked on it over three days, probably 8 or 9 hours total. that is just the metalwork, not counting the time he spent choosing the stone and designing the piece in his head

So right off the bat, materials are around $75. At $180, that leaves $105 for 8-9 hours of skilled labor — that's about $12 an hour. For a trained silversmith doing custom work with quality stone. That's less than minimum wage in most states.

A reasonable way to price it: materials ($75) + labor (let's say $35-50/hour for skilled artisan work, so $280-450) + a markup for artistry, overhead, and the fact that it's handmade and one-of-a-kind. That puts the bracelet somewhere between $400 and $600 depending on how aggressive you want to be.
see that is what i told him. but he says the tourists at Gallup see a price tag over $300 and they walk away. they go buy the machine-made stuff from the import shops instead

That's a real tension. But here's the thing — the people buying $30 machine-made turquoise jewelry were never going to buy his work anyway. They're two completely different markets. The customer who appreciates a handmade Kingman turquoise bracelet by a Navajo silversmith is not the same person browsing the import tables.

Has he thought about having a few lower-priced items to draw people in — simple rings or pendants — and then the higher-end pieces for serious buyers? That way he's not leaving the casual shoppers empty-handed, but he's also not giving away his best work.

now that is smart. the rings he can price lower. they take maybe an hour each and the stones are smaller. so $60 to $80 for a ring, that gets people to the table. then they see the bracelet and the bolos and if they understand the work they will pay for it

Exactly — the rings are the gateway. They bring foot traffic to the table, and once someone picks up the bracelet and feels the weight and sees the detail, the price makes sense to them. You can also have a small card or sign that says something about the process — how long it takes, that the stone is genuine Kingman, that it's hand-fabricated. Educating the buyer justifies the price without Raymond having to do a sales pitch for every piece.

he will not do a sales pitch, i can tell you that right now. the man barely talks to me some days and i have been married to him forever. a little card is a good idea though. i could write that up for him

Ha — the quiet artist type. That's actually fine, a lot of buyers prefer that. It feels more authentic than a hard sell. A simple card that says something like: "Handcrafted by Raymond Begay, Navajo silversmith. Genuine Kingman turquoise, sterling silver, hand-fabricated. Each piece is one of a kind." Short and real. Let the work speak for itself, and the card fills in what Raymond won't say out loud.
okay so what should the bolos be priced at. they are nice pieces, silver tips, good stones, probably 4 to 5 hours of work each

For a bolo tie with quality stones and silver tips, 4-5 hours of work — I'd say $250 to $350 range depending on the stone. Figure materials are maybe $40-60, labor at $40/hour is $160-200, plus the artisan markup. A well-made bolo with genuine turquoise from a Navajo silversmith at Gallup? That's worth $300 and the right buyer knows it.

If he has two, maybe price them slightly different based on the stones. Gives the buyer a choice without haggling.

okay. i am going to write all this down and show it to him tonight. he will grumble about it but he knows i am right. he always prices with his heart and i price with the calculator. we balance each other out, you know

That's a good partnership — the artist and the business manager. Show him the hourly math. When he sees he's paying himself $12 an hour for master-level work, that might shift his thinking. His hands are worth more than that, and so is his time. Good luck with the conversation tonight.
ahéhee'. i will let you know how the grumbling goes. he is a good man he just does not know his own worth sometimes. okay i have to go start dinner before he comes in from the workshop hungry
Tell him a stranger on the internet says his bracelet is worth at least $400. Sometimes it takes an outside voice. Enjoy dinner, and good luck at Gallup with the new pricing.
